mcqs.question: Which of the following is the correct way to open a file for writing in C++?

options.text: fstream file("data.txt", ios::write);
ofstream file("data.txt", ios::out);
file.open("data.txt", input);
fstream file = open("data.txt", write);

mcqs.explanation: ofstream is used for writing, and ios::out is the default mode for ofstream, making it the correct way to open a file for writing.

mcqs.question: What happens when you open a file with ios::app mode?

options.text: The file is overwritten with new content
Data is appended at the end of the file, and the file is created if it doesn't exist
The file pointer is moved to the beginning for reading
Data can only be written at the beginning of the file

mcqs.explanation: ios::app (append mode) moves the write pointer to the end of the file and creates the file if it doesn't exist, preserving existing content.

mcqs.question: To write binary data to a file in C++, which file mode should be combined with ios::out?

options.text: ios::text
ios::binary
ios::data
ios::bin

mcqs.explanation: ios::binary is used to open a file in binary mode, which writes data in its raw binary form without any text formatting.

mcqs.question: After writing data to a file using ofstream, you want to read from the same file. What should you do?

options.text: Use the same ofstream object to read
Close the file and reopen it in input mode using ifstream
Use seekg(0) to move to the beginning
Call clear() method and use ios::in mode

mcqs.explanation: ofstream is designed for output only; to read from the same file, you need to close it and reopen using ifstream in input mode.

mcqs.question: Which function is used to check if a file operation has failed due to a logical error (such as opening a non-existent file)?

options.text: file.bad()
file.fail()
file.eof()
file.error()

mcqs.explanation: fail() returns true when a logical I/O error occurs, such as failing to open a file or reading/writing data incorrectly.

mcqs.question: What does seekg(0, ios::end) do in C++ file handling?

options.text: Moves the get pointer to the beginning of the file
Moves the get pointer to the end of the file
Moves the put pointer to the beginning of the file
Returns the current position of the get pointer

mcqs.explanation: seekg(offset, ios::end) moves the get (read) pointer to a position relative to the end of the file; seekg(0, ios::end) moves to the end.

mcqs.question: Why is it important to close a file after operations are complete in C++?

options.text: It is not necessary to close files in C++
To ensure all data is flushed from the buffer to the file and release system resources
Closing files makes the program run faster
It prevents the file from being opened again

mcqs.explanation: Closing a file flushes the output buffer to ensure all data is written to disk and releases the file handle resources back to the system.

mcqs.question: If you want to open a file for both reading and writing in binary mode, which fstream mode combination is correct?

options.text: ios::in | ios::out
ios::in | ios::out | ios::binary
ios::read | ios::write | ios::binary
ios::both | ios::binary

mcqs.explanation: fstream with ios::in | ios::out | ios::binary allows both reading and writing in binary mode, using a single file stream object.
